text:
THORACIC ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Margaret Tran | DOB on file | MRN on file
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Visit type: In-person follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Three-month follow-up for stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant right lower lobe lung adenocarcinoma on osimertinib (Tagrisso). Restaging imaging reviewed today.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Tran is a 62-year-old Vietnamese-American woman and former smoker (12 pack-years, quit ~18 years ago) with a history of well-controlled hypertension and hypothyroidism who was diagnosed approximately 3 months ago with stage IIIB (T3N2M0) lung adenocarcinoma of the right lower lobe. Her initial presentation was progressive exertional dyspnea over 6 weeks, right-sided chest discomfort, and roughly 4 kg of unintentional weight loss. CT chest demonstrated a 4.8 cm spiculated RLL mass with ipsilateral mediastinal and subcarinal adenopathy (stations 4R, 7). PET confirmed FDG-avid disease at these sites without distant metastases (SUVmax 14.2 primary; 9.8 and 11.3 at 4R and subcarinal nodes, respectively). Brain MRI was negative for intracranial disease at staging.

CT-guided transthoracic biopsy of the RLL mass revealed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with acinar and lepidic growth patterns, TTF-1 positive, Napsin A positive, PD-L1 TPS 15% by 22C3. Comprehensive tissue NGS (523-gene panel) identified an EGFR exon 21 L858R activating mutation (AF 34.2%) with co-mutant TP53 p.R175H. ALK, ROS1, MET, KRAS, ERBB2, and RET alterations were all negative/absent. TMB low at 6.2 mutations/Mb, MSS.

Given the EGFR-driver mutation and patient preference to avoid upfront chemoradiation toxicity, the multidisciplinary tumor board elected to proceed with osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally daily as first-line therapy, with discussion of the LAURA trial data. She initiated osimertinib approximately 3 months ago.

Since starting Tagrisso, Ms. Tran has noted the following:
- Significant improvement in exertional dyspnea. She notes she can now walk her usual distance without stopping and is no longer breathless during routine classroom activities.
- Right chest discomfort has resolved.
- Weight: she has regained approximately 2 kg. Currently back to near her prior weight.
- Skin: she developed a grade 2 acneiform rash involving the face, neck, and upper chest approximately 6 weeks after starting Tagrisso. This was managed with oral doxycycline 100 mg BID and topical clindamycin 1% gel. She reports the rash has substantially improved and she would now describe it as mild -- scattered papules on the forehead and chin, not painful, not cosmetically distressing. She has been compliant with the topical regimen and is pleased with the response to doxycycline.
- Paronychia of the right index and middle fingers (grade 1 at last visit) has resolved. She is soaking her nails as directed.
- Diarrhea: grade 1 loose stools (2-3 episodes above baseline per day) have continued but are manageable with loperamide as needed; she uses it approximately 2-3 times per week.
- No new complaints of cough, hemoptysis, or fever.
- No headaches, visual changes, or neurological symptoms.
- No new joint pains or musculoskeletal complaints.
- Mood: the patient reports she is feeling much more optimistic than at diagnosis. She has returned to full-time teaching and finds the work meaningful. She is sleeping reasonably well.

Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast was performed this week and reviewed in clinic today. The right lower lobe mass has decreased from 4.8 cm to 2.9 cm -- roughly a 40% reduction. Mediastinal and subcarinal lymphadenopathy at stations 4R and 7 is markedly decreased and now sub-centimeter bilaterally. No new pulmonary nodules, no pleural effusion, no hepatic or adrenal involvement identified.

Overall, she is doing very well on Tagrisso at 3 months with an excellent radiographic and symptomatic response.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive for: mild intermittent loose stools (managed, grade 1), mild residual papulopustular rash on face (grade 1, improving), mild fatigue at end of school day (not significantly limiting).
Negative for: hemoptysis, significant cough, new or worsening dyspnea, chest pain, fever, chills, night sweats, significant anorexia, nausea, vomiting, constipation, abdominal pain, headache, dizziness, visual changes, focal weakness, sensory changes, bone pain, rash elsewhere, palpitations, peripheral edema.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vitals: BP 128/76 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 96% on room air, weight 58 kg (up from 54 kg at initiation), temperature 36.8°C.
General: Well-appearing, alert, in no acute distress. Speaks fluent English. Accompanied by her daughter.
HEENT: Scattered papules and mild erythema on forehead and bilateral cheeks consistent with grade 1 acneiform rash. No pustules or crust. No alopecia.
Neck/skin: Scattered papules along the neck, fewer than at last visit. No new skin lesions.
Lymph nodes: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy.
Lungs: Breath sounds clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or focal dullness. Good air movement throughout.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs or rubs. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, nontender, nondistended. No hepatosplenomegaly. Normoactive bowel sounds.
Extremities: No clubbing. Nails on right index and middle fingers appear healed without active paronychia. No edema.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No focal deficits.
Performance status: ECOG 1 (improved from ECOG 1 at initiation and compared to presentation when more symptomatic).

---

LAB RESULTS
CBC: WBC 6.8 K/uL (normal differential), Hgb 12.4 g/dL (mild, asymptomatic, stable), Plt 218 K/uL.
CMP: Sodium 139, potassium 4.1, bicarbonate 24, BUN 14, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, glucose 98, calcium 9.2. AST 28 U/L, ALT 22 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 76 U/L, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL.
TSH: 2.8 mIU/L (stable on current levothyroxine dose).
No QTc prolongation on most recent EKG obtained at 6-week visit (432 ms baseline, patient declined repeat today given no new symptoms).

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (reviewed today):
- The right lower lobe mass has shrunk substantially, down from 4.8 cm at diagnosis to approximately 2.9 cm. The mass is less spiculated in appearance, with improved surrounding ground-glass infiltrate.
- Mediastinal and subcarinal lymph nodes at stations 4R and 7 are markedly reduced and now appear sub-centimeter. The previously bulky 4R node complex is barely discernible.
- No new pulmonary nodules or masses. No pleural effusion.
- Liver, adrenal glands, and remainder of the abdomen and pelvis unremarkable. No new sites of disease.
- Overall: Impressive response to Tagrisso at 3 months. The primary tumor and nodal disease are clearly responding well.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IIIB (T3N2M0) EGFR L858R-mutant RLL lung adenocarcinoma, currently on first-line osimertinib (Tagrisso 80 mg daily) -- on treatment, responding.
   - Restaging CT demonstrates excellent partial response at 3 months. Primary mass reduced from 4.8 cm to 2.9 cm; mediastinal nodes markedly down-trending and sub-centimeter.
   - Clinically, patient reports resolution of dyspnea and chest discomfort, weight recovery, and return to full-time teaching. ECOG PS 1 (functionally closer to 0 in her routine activities).
   - PLAN: Continue osimertinib 80 mg orally daily. No dose modification warranted at this time. Next restaging imaging at approximately 6 months from treatment initiation (i.e., approximately 3 months from today). Lab work (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH) to be rechecked at 6-month visit or sooner if symptoms develop.

2. Grade 1 acneiform/papulopustular rash (EGFR-related, improving from grade 2).
   - Rash has responded nicely to doxycycline 100 mg BID plus topical clindamycin 1% gel. Now grade 1.
   - PLAN: Discontinue doxycycline today given sustained improvement to grade 1. Continue topical clindamycin 1% gel daily to affected areas of face and neck. Patient to continue gentle non-comedogenic moisturizer and mineral-based sunscreen. If rash worsens back to grade 2, resume doxycycline. Instruct patient to avoid excessive sun exposure.

3. Grade 1 diarrhea (EGFR-related).
   - Manageable with loperamide PRN 2-3 times per week. No nutritional or hydration impact.
   - PLAN: Continue loperamide as needed. Patient counseled on adequate hydration. BRAT diet reinforcement if worsening. If escalates to grade 2-3, will reassess Tagrisso dosing.

4. Paronychia -- resolved. No active paronychia at today's visit. Continue nail soaks as maintenance hygiene.

5. Hypertension -- well-controlled. BP 128/76 today on amlodipine 5 mg daily. No changes needed.

6. Hypothyroidism -- well-controlled. TSH 2.8 on levothyroxine 88 mcg daily. No changes needed.

Current medications:
- Osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally daily
- Levothyroxine 88 mcg orally daily (taken on empty stomach)
- Amlodipine 5 mg orally daily
- Folic acid (patient asked about supplementation; not required for Tagrisso, but she is taking a multivitamin -- acceptable)
- Topical clindamycin 1% gel to face/neck daily
- Loperamide 2 mg PO PRN diarrhea
- Doxycycline 100 mg BID -- DISCONTINUED today

Return to clinic: approximately 3 months (or sooner if new symptoms, rash worsening, or concerns arise). Restaging imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis +/- brain MRI if indicated by symptoms) to be ordered prior to next visit. Patient instructed to call triage line for any new neurological symptoms, hemoptysis, significant dyspnea, rash flare, or GI concerns.

Time spent with patient and family in counseling and coordination: approximately 40 minutes.

Documented and attested by: [Attending Thoracic Oncologist]
Department of Medical Oncology, Thoracic Oncology Program
